raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "All public high schools in California will be required to offer at least one computer science education course.",
    "Computer science will be established as a high school graduation requirement in California by the 2030-31 school year.",
    "Computer science skills are needed in many career fields, including science, technology, agriculture, entertainment, fashion, banking, and marketing.",
    "California has 45,245 open computing jobs with an average salary of $153,544 as of January 2023.",
    "In 2020, there were only 9,339 graduates in computer science in California.",
    "California has the highest number of open computing jobs in the country.",
    "Fifty-five percent of high schools in California do not offer a single course in computer science.",
    "Only 5 percent of the 1,930,000 high school pupils in California are enrolled in a computer science course.",
    "Only 34 percent of schools serving high proportions of Black, Indigenous, Latinx, and Pacific Islander pupils offer computer science courses, compared to 52 percent of schools serving a greater proportion of White and Asian pupils.",
    "Female pupils comprise 49 percent of the high school population, but only 30 percent of pupils taking computer science courses are female.",
    "Schools serving low-income communities are three times less likely to offer core computer science courses compared to schools serving high-income communities.",
    "Rural schools are two times less likely to offer computer science courses than urban schools.",
    "California lags behind the national average and 40 other states in the percentage of high schools offering at least one computer science course."
  ]
}
```
